title: Filtering Resources with Field Selectors
description:
## Filtering Resources with Field Selectors

Field selectors let you filter Kubernetes resources by the **value of specific object fields** — similar to how label selectors work but targeting any field in the resource spec or status.

Common use cases:
- Find all pods in a specific phase: `kubectl get pods --field-selector=status.phase=Running`
- Find resources in a specific namespace: `kubectl get pods --field-selector=metadata.namespace=kube-system`

**Your task:**

Several pods have been created for you — some Running, some in a Failed state.

1. Use a field selector to list only the **Running** pods and count them. Save the count to `/tmp/running-count.txt`
2. Use a field selector to list all pods **not** in the `default` namespace (filter by `metadata.namespace!=default`)

```bash
# Count running pods:
kubectl get pods --field-selector=status.phase=Running --no-headers | wc -l
```

hints.title: Field selector syntax
hints.body: Use `--field-selector=field.path=value` to filter. Multiple conditions are comma-separated. Supported operators: `=`, `==`, `!=`.

hints.title: Save running pod count to a file
hints.body: Pipe the result through wc -l and redirect to the file.

commands.description: File /tmp/running-count.txt exists and has a numeric value
The saved count matches the actual number of Running pods
commands.command: LIVE=$(kubectl get pods --field-selector=status.phase=Running --no-headers 2>/dev/null | wc -l | tr -d ' '); SAVED=$(cat /tmp/running-count.txt 2>/dev/null | tr -d ' '); [ "$LIVE" = "$SAVED" ] && echo 'match' || echo 'mismatch'

teardown_commands.command: kubectl delete pod alpha beta crash-pod --ignore-not-found --grace-period=0 --force
